steps.title: Identify the problem rail must solve
steps.content: Rail's advantage over short-haul flights is the absence of lead time (no security, baggage, or boarding delays). But the passage adds a crucial "However": as rail passenger volumes approach capacity limits, long lead times emerge for rail too. This erases rail's advantage. The blank must describe what rail needs to do to prevent those capacity-driven lead times.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• "rail systems should offer fewer long-haul routes and airlines should offer more long-haul routes.": The passage never discusses long-haul routes for either mode; there is no basis for this inference. ✗
  • "rail systems may need to schedule additional trains for these routes.": Adding trains expands capacity, preventing the congestion that generates long lead times and keeping rail competitive with short-haul flights. ✓
  • "security, baggage handling, and boarding procedures used by airlines may need to be implemented for rail systems.": Adopting airline procedures would create the long lead times rail currently avoids — the opposite of solving the problem. ✗
  • "passengers who travel by rail for these routes will need to accept that lead times will be similar to those for air travel.": Accepting worse conditions does not keep rail "viable" as an alternative; it concedes the advantage rail needs to retain. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
